MOR Health Analysis: Examining MOUD Reciept and Incaraceration Statistics

Biostatistician: Licza Lobo, Jacob Parquet

Principal Investigator: Ali Jalai, PhD; Mila Sun, PhD; Caroline Andy

July 17, 2026

Study Background Information

Individuals with opioid use disorder are at high risk of adverse healthcare outcomes and overdose upon jail release. Incarceration increases overdose risk due to reduced opioid tolerance, interrupted social support, and limited access to treatment during confinement. Medication for opioid use disorder (MOUD) offered during incarceration may mitigate this risk: a 2025 study of individuals released from Massachusetts county jails found that receipt of MOUD in jail was associated with a greater likelihood of postrelease MOUD initiation, along with a 52% lower risk of fatal opioid overdose, a 24% lower risk of non-fatal opioid overdose, a 56% lower risk of death from any cause, and a 12% lower risk of reincarceration after release. Provision of MOUD during incarceration has also been associated with decreased emergency department visits, increased MOUD uptake, and increased Medicaid outpatient and pharmacy claims post-release from prison.

Aims

Explore the association between medication for opioid use disorder upon jail release and time to the next incarceration event. Examine whether providing medication for opioid use disorder upon jail release reduces acute care (emergency department and inpatient visit) cost within the month following release.

Results & Comments

Our findings suggest that studies examining vulnerable populations, such as individuals with OUD, are particularly susceptible to bias from unmeasured confounding. In the present study, prior literature suggested that individuals who received MOUD upon release from prison would have a longer interval before reincarceration than those who did not receive MOUD. Contrary to this hypothesis, our crude (unadjusted) analysis found the opposite: individuals who received MOUD were reincarcerated sooner than those who did not. Propensity score matching, used to balance measured covariates and account for differences in severity, produced results that were essentially unchanged from the crude analysis. To address unmeasured confounding directly, we implemented a self-matched design comparing incarceration events within the same individual, one following MOUD receipt and one without. This self-matched analysis reversed the earlier finding and instead showed that MOUD receipt at release was associated with a longer time to reincarceration, consistent with prior literature. The same pattern emerged when we applied this approach to acute care costs in the first month post-release. The crude model suggested that individuals who received MOUD incurred higher costs than those who did not; however, the self-matched design reversed this relationship, showing that MOUD recipients incurred lower post-release costs. Together, these results suggest that self-matched designs may better isolate true treatment effects by removing bias from unmeasured confounding in ways that crude and propensity-matched approaches cannot. Further research is needed to establish the statistical theory and assumptions underlying this method, and to test its performance across other vulnerable populations.

Division of Biostatistics

Department of Population Health Science

Licza Lobo, Jacob Parquet, Biostatistics Internship 2026
